https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898.note:
Really large cohort, although the at-home collection is tricky especially for metatranscriptomics, as RNA half-life is very short in bacteria. Would love a more detailed description of the collection methodologies, they just refer to a previous paper.
Regarding analysis, they have a custom collection of genomes of diverse sources, including NCBI and UHGG (are these genomes or pan genomes?)
https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898.title: The human gut microbiome activity is resilient and stable for up to six months: a large stool metatranscriptomic study

https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898.note: Multiple cohorts. seems that in the human data no association between microbiota at first months of life and cognitive outcomes, and most of the observed associations are in mice after fecal transplantation experiments from the human samples
https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.64898.title: Infant gut microbiomes contribute to metabolic states that impact brain function

https://doi.org/10.1186/s12967-026-07968.note: Fusobacterium + lactate metabolism. I think I've seen this before, either as a preprint or as "advance access".
https://doi.org/10.1186/s12967-026-07968.title: Fusobacterium nucleatum promotes tumor progression driven by histone lactylation through increasing GLUT1 expression in

https://doi.org/10.1007/s10552-026-02144.note: Very interesting, related to association != causation between microbiome and CRC. From the discussion: "we did not observe significant associations with the species most consistently linked to CRC, such as Fusobacterium nucleatum and enterotoxigenic Bacteroides fragilis [4, 5]. This may be due to variation in microbial composition over the progression of carcinogenesis. For example, in a study of 616 Japanese adults, F. nucleatum was linked particularly with advanced CRC stages [72]. In a study of 500 German adults, F. nucleatum was associated with CRC but neither with pre-cancerous lesions in the gut nor with diet or other lifestyle factors [77]."
https://doi.org/10.1007/s10552-026-02144.title: Interplay between colorectal cancer-related lifestyles and the gut microbiome: an exploratory analysis of metagenomic da

https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cmet.2026.02.0.note: Large cohort and deep phenotyping, with a lot of metadata available. Interesting to see how composition and function may be associated to similar or different host features
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cmet.2026.02.0.title: Large-scale metaproteomics of human gut microbiota reveals microbial functions in metabolic diseases and aging.

https://doi.org/10.1186/s40168-026-02378.note: I'm starting to see more papers and preprints on "bacterial consortia" and how they interact to contribute to CRC development
https://doi.org/10.1186/s40168-026-02378.title: The interplay between Peptostreptococcus and Fusobacterium as novel signatures in colorectal cancer recurrence.
